Value: Single reference sheet of ALL configuration values and data inputs of the model, with explicit sources. The executable configuration lives in model_config.py (tunable values) and lng_data.py (supply-side data); update sheet and code together when changing a value.

Data = taken from a cited source; Derived = computed from Data; Calibrated = fitted to observed 2025-26 prices in the calibration loop; Assumption = stylised modelling choice; Numerical = solver/algorithm setting with no economic content

Source / Justification: Model start Sep 2025: six pre-closure months to establish the baseline equilibrium
Closure begins March 2026 -- realized event date (TTF +55% in two trading days from 2 March 2026, Kpler / press reports)
Scenario assumption: 6-month closure (Mar-Aug 2026), within the 5-9 month range of historical energy-chokepoint disruptions
Horizon extension so the terminal-storage equality does not contaminate reported results at t<=+12 (standard fix for terminal-condition artefacts: Conejo, Carrion & Morales 2010, ch. 3)
Headline results reported through Feb 2027 only; t=+13..+24 are a dynamic-programming tail

Prior on monthly closure-arrival rate, mean 2/42 = 4.8%/month. Fitted to the observed FLAT pre-crisis TTF (~33-38 EUR/MWh Sep 2025 - Feb 2026): the market priced essentially no imminent-closure premium and was surprised on 2 March (Kpler). Framework: Grenadier & Malenko (2010, J. Finance); Bouri et al. (2020, Energy Economics)
Prior on monthly reopening rate, mean 2/14 = 14.3%/month, i.e. expected closure duration ~7 months -- consistent with historical closure durations of major supply disruptions (~5-9 months)

Posterior mean used as branching probability at every tree node; one count added per monthly observation (Grenadier & Malenko 2010; Hou & Nguyen 2018, Energy Economics, for the gas-market Markov-switching precedent)

All Hormuz-transiting LNG stranded: 93% of Qatari and 96% of UAE LNG shipments transit Hormuz; production (BEP) and untouched routes unchanged
Two-leader EPEC; Gulf = composite of the Hormuz-stranded exporters (jointly ~20% of global LNG exports)
Both face identical blocking exposure (93%/96% Hormuz transit shares), so aggregation is the natural coalition
Capacity-weighted composite BEP differs by < $0.03/MMBtu from Qatar's (UAE ~4% of composite capacity)
= Qatar 8.83 + UAE 0.67; zero at every CLOSED tree node

US liquefaction ran at ~full utilisation through 2025-26 (EIA LNG export data); 'USA' aggregates many competing private exporters with individual dispatch incentives, so unified withholding power is small. Calibration v3
QatarEnergy portfolio ~85% long-term contracted (GIIGNL Annual Report); only the residual is discretionary spot. Floor binds TOTAL dispatch, cross-basin allocation stays strategic. Calibration v3
Full nameplate capacity spot-tradable (former Hypothesis H1 removed)
Stylised destination-market access reflecting historical trade patterns (IEA gas-market reports); fringe is price-taking, restored in calibration v2 to discipline the strategic markup
As above; Oman is Hormuz-adjacent but exports from outside the strait, kept in fringe yet blocked during closure per event definition
Demoted leader kept as Asian price-taking fringe (Asia-locked strategic decision is degenerate)
Demoted leader kept as Asian price-taking fringe
Norwegian pipeline exports ~120 bcm/yr = ~10 bcm/month (IEA); cost round-number estimate
Transmed/Medgaz flows ~45-50 bcm/yr capacity, ~4 bcm/month utilised; cost round-number estimate
Round-number estimate for Russian pipeline gas to NE Asia

EU staircase approximates inverse demand P = 220 - 5.26*Q over Q in [24,40] bcm, fitted to two observed (quantity, price) pairs straddling the closure: (Jan 2026: 49 bcm @ EUR 37) and (Mar 2026: 35 bcm @ EUR 57) from eu_demand_monthly.csv + calibration_targets.csv. Slope ~ -5.3 EUR/MWh per bcm = inelastic short-run gas demand (Burke & Yang 2016). Block 1 = essential heating + petrochemical at the rationing ceiling
Larger base (~44 bcm addressable), shallower response than EU; pre-crisis clears EUR 30-38, crisis EUR 48-68 (JKM $15-24/MMBtu range, calibration_targets.csv)

Tail re-anchored to Fulwood (2026, OIES): Indian demand destruction starts only at TTF ~$25/MMBtu (~EUR 79); South Asia + China imports fall just 3-18 bcm/YEAR in severe scenarios, so the deep tail is small (calibration v6)

Unit: multiplier on the essential heating block ONLY (v6.2)
Source / Justification: Observed EU monthly gas demand 2023-2025 normalised to the annual mean (~28.2 bcm/month); source: eu_demand_monthly.csv (user-provided Eurostat-based series)
Flatter than EU: Japan/Korea heating partially offset by flat Chinese industrial demand
EU aggregate working gas volume ~100 bcm (GIE AGSI+)
Observed EU storage fill at model start (Sep 2025) ~85% (GIE AGSI+)
Typical end-of-winter EU storage level, imposed at t=+24 (Feb 2028) -- far outside the reported window
Stylised: NE-Asian LNG tank capacity is small relative to EU underground storage
EU Regulation 2017/1938 as amended by Reg. (EU) 2022/1032: 90% storage-filling target on 1 November, applied at every realized November node
Observed front-month TTF and JKM monthly averages (JKM converted USD/MMBtu -> EUR/MWh); see calibration_targets.csv; market reports LSEG/ICE/Platts via press, retrieved June 2026
TTF +55% in two trading days after 2 March (Kpler); monthly average
Ceasefire-expectation dip (belief-driven; the open/closed information structure cannot reproduce it endogenously -- documented model limitation)

Fortuny-Amat & McCarl (1981) disjunctive linearisation of KKT complementarity; M_PRICE must exceed the maximum plausible nodal price (max WTP 120), M_FRINGE/M_DEMAND exceed max monthly quantities, M_KKT exceeds any stationarity expression, M_STORAGE exceeds S_max
Bilinear leader objective pi*q -> nonconvex MIQCP
Loose 3% gap during iterations: the damped update only uses the best response directionally; final storage-extraction solve uses a tighter gap
Gauss-Seidel best-response rounds; rolling needs fewer because the equilibrium changes only incrementally month to month
Convergence when max |dq| < 0.5 bcm across leaders/regions/nodes
Roll window Sep 2025 - Feb 2027; each monthly re-solve still plans to T_LAST=+24, so even the last implemented month has a 12-month lookahead
Few iterations + moderate time limit per monthly re-solve: the damped equilibrium changes only incrementally month to month
Gradual restart after reopening: Ras Laffan 2-4 weeks restart + 1-2 weeks ramp, Das Island ~3 weeks (Fulwood 2026, OIES Energy Comment) -- first open month delivers ~half capacity
Two damaged Ras Laffan trains offline until ~2031 (~1.3 of 15.1 bcm/month) (Fulwood 2026, OIES)
Crisis-year 1-Nov target: EU flexibility mechanism; Fulwood (2026, OIES) projects 76-81 bcm on 1 Nov 2026 -- the regular 90% is unattainable
EU-27 stocks 30 bcm at end of March 2026: 6 bcm lower y/y and 16 bcm below the 2022-25 end-March average (Fulwood 2026, OIES / GIE AGSI+)

Non-LNG supply aggregate (v6.1): EU domestic production ~3 + UK net flows ~2 + Azerbaijan TAP ~1 + Turkstream ~1.3 + Libya ~0.2 + biomethane ~1.5 (IEA Gas Market Report 2026). Needed because demand staircases are calibrated to TOTAL demand while the 2026 LNG fleet replaced the oversized 2040 capacities that had accidentally covered this supply
Non-LNG supply aggregate (v6.1): Chinese pipeline imports ONLY: Central Asia ~4 + Power of Siberia ~3.5 + Myanmar ~0.3 (IEA, v7); domestic production cannot surge into the seaborne spot market

Available vs nameplate LNG capacity: maintenance, technical outages, feedgas constraints (Fulwood 2024, OIES NG 195, fn. 3 + Fig. 4 -- the ~98% pre-crisis utilisation is of AVAILABLE capacity). Applied to all LNG suppliers, not pipelines/non-LNG aggregates (v7)

Minimum operational storage level (security cushion) in every month; storage may not be drawn below it even under scarcity. 30%: Fulwood (2026, OIES) reports EU storage at 30 bcm = 30% of working capacity at end-March 2026, the observed low point. Simple data-anchored proxy for precautionary storage without a risk measure (v7.1)
